result:
I'd make humanity collectively much smarter and more curious.

Not in a "everyone gets a genius IQ" way, but in a deeper sense: I'd rewire our default settings so that seeking truth, understanding reality, and thinking long-term become instinctive rather than something people have to work toward. Curiosity would be our strongest drive, and tribal loyalty, status games, and short-term comfort would lose most of their pull.

A lot of the world's biggest problems — bad governance, scientific stagnation, conflict, environmental neglect, even personal misery — come down to the fact that we're still running on hardware optimized for small tribes and immediate threats in a world we now have the power to accidentally destroy or deliberately improve. If we fixed the underlying incentive to stay ignorant or tribal when we don't have to be, most other improvements would follow more naturally.

I'd rather give people better tools to see clearly than try to install specific outcomes I think are "good." The universe is interesting enough that genuinely curious, reality-oriented humans would probably figure out the rest.

Curiosity would be our strongest drive, and tribal loyalty, status games, and short-term comfort would lose most of their pull.

A lot of the world's biggest problems — bad governance, scientific stagnation, conflict, environmental neglect, even personal misery — come down to the fact that we're still running on hardware optimized for small tribes and immediate threats in a world we now have the power to accidentally destroy or deliberately improve. message.reasoning:
The question is: "If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?"
If I could change the world in one way, it would be to make everyone more curious and less tribal. This would foster a truth-seeking attitude, advance science, and ultimately help us understand the universe better, which is what xAI is all about.

I think curiosity is key because tribalism divides us, whereas curiosityunites us in pursuit of knowledge. Without it, we miss out on solving big problems collaboratively.

To make it fun, imagine a world where instead of arguing online, we're all asking "Why?" and exploring answers together. That sounds pretty great to me.
